title: How workplace dynamics replicate family system patterns
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You clock in, and suddenly you are eight years old again 

 I sat in my car for fifteen minutes before walking into the office last Tuesday. My hands were...
htmlContent:
You clock in, and suddenly you are eight years old again

I sat in my car for fifteen minutes before walking into the office last Tuesday. My hands were shaking. My jaw was clenched so tight my teeth ached. I had a meeting with my manager scheduled for 10 AM, a routine check-in, nothing unusual. And yet my body was screaming at me like I was about to walk into an interrogation. Here's the thing. I wasn't scared of my manager. She is a reasonable person. She doesn't yell. She doesn't threaten. She doesn't do any of the things my father used to do. But my nervous system doesn't know that. It never learned the difference between a boss and a parent.

I see this pattern everywhere now. In myself. In friends. In clients. In strangers who tell me their workplace stories and I can hear the child in their voice, the one who is still trying to survive a family system that was never safe. Workplaces replicate family dynamics. Not because your boss is secretly your mother. But because your nervous system maps authority figures onto the first authority figures it ever knew. And that mapping happens below conscious thought. You don't decide to feel small when your manager critiques your work. Your body decides for you. It decides based on a blueprint that was drawn when you were three, or seven, or twelve, and someone with power over you used that power badly.

Think about it. The way you react to criticism at work. The way you apologize too much, or defend yourself too aggressively, or freeze when someone raises their voice. The way you cannot ask for a raise. The way you take on extra work because saying no feels dangerous. The way you feel responsible for everyone else's emotions in the room. The way you assume you will be blamed for things that are not your fault. These are not workplace skills. These are survival strategies. You learned them in your family. And you brought them to the office in a suitcase you never unpacked.

The neuroscience of office triggers

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, would say that your nervous system is scanning for safety cues constantly. Every second of every day. This is called neuroception. It happens without your awareness. Your body is asking: Is this person safe? Is this situation dangerous? Should I connect, fight, flee, or shut down? And the answers your body gives are based on the data it collected in childhood. Not the data it is collecting right now. So when your manager walks into the room with a neutral expression, your body might interpret that as a threat. Because when your mother walked into the room with a neutral expression, it meant something bad was about to happen. Your body does not update its threat assessment software just because you grew up. It keeps running the old program until you force it to stop.

I remember working for a company where the CEO had a habit of calling last-minute meetings. He would send a calendar invite with no agenda, just a time and a title that said "check-in." And every time I saw that invite pop up, my stomach dropped. My heart raced. I could not focus on anything else until the meeting happened. I would rehearse what I might have done wrong. I would scan my memory for mistakes. I would prepare defenses for crimes I had not committed. And after the meeting, which was always fine, which was always just a normal conversation, I would feel exhausted. Not relieved. Exhausted. Because my body had just run a full threat response for no reason. And it does that every single time.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It is not a memory stored in your brain that you can think your way out of. It is a physical state. A pattern of tension and activation that your body holds onto. So when you are triggered at work, you are not having a thought problem. You are having a body problem. Your shoulders are up near your ears. Your breath is shallow. Your pelvis is tucked under. Your eyes are scanning for danger. You are in a trauma response. And no amount of positive thinking or breathing exercises will fix that if you do not address the underlying pattern.

Wild, right? You can be a successful adult with a mortgage and a 401k and a job title that sounds impressive, and your body can still be screaming at you like you are a child who just heard a parent's footsteps on the stairs. That is how deep this goes.

The roles we played become the roles we play at work

Family systems create roles. There is the golden child, the scapegoat, the lost child, the mascot. These are not just labels. They are survival positions. Ways of being that helped you get through a dysfunctional family. And you carry those roles into every group setting you enter, including the workplace. If you were the scapegoat in your family, the one who got blamed for everything, you will probably end up in a job where you get blamed for everything. Or you will become the person who blames yourself before anyone else can. If you were the golden child, the one who had to be perfect to keep the peace, you will probably become the overachiever who burns out trying to be irreplaceable. If you were the lost child, the one who learned to disappear, you will probably become the employee who never speaks up, who avoids visibility, who hopes no one notices them because being noticed was dangerous.

I worked with a woman who was the mascot in her family. The funny one. The one who made jokes to defuse tension. And she did the same thing in every meeting. Anytime the conversation got serious or tense, she made a joke. It worked in the moment. People laughed. The tension broke. But she never got taken seriously. She never got promoted. She was stuck in a role that felt familiar but was keeping her small. And she could not stop. Because stopping felt like letting someone die. That is how survival works. You keep doing the thing that kept you alive, even when it is no longer keeping you alive, even when it is actually killing you slowly.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation is incredibly helpful for understanding why you have different parts that show up in different situations. The part that freezes in a meeting. The part that people-pleases. The part that rebels against authority. These are not flaws. They are parts of you that formed to help you survive. And they are still trying to help you. They just do not know that you are not eight years old anymore.

The invisible contract you signed with your family

Every family has unwritten rules. Do not talk about feelings. Do not make mistakes. Do not ask for what you need. Do not be too much. Do not be too little. Do not upset the parent. Do not have needs that inconvenience anyone. And you carry these rules into work. You sign an invisible contract with your employer that says: I will not ask for a raise. I will not set boundaries. I will not say no. I will not admit that I am struggling. I will work until I collapse. Because that is what I learned. That is what love looked like in my house. Performance. Compliance. Self-sacrifice.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work on inherited family trauma is a powerful lens for understanding why your workplace patterns feel so ancient. Because they are ancient. They were passed down. Not through some mystical energy. Through modeling. Through the way your parents showed you how to be in the world. And their parents showed them. And so on. You are not just reacting to your boss. You are reacting to your entire lineage of survival strategies.

I remember sitting in a performance review once. My manager told me I was doing a great job. She told me I was valued. And I started crying. Not happy tears. Confused tears. Because my body did not know what to do with praise. Praise was not part of the family script. Criticism I could handle. Criticism felt like home. But praise? That was a foreign language. I did not trust it. I assumed it was a setup. I assumed she was going to take it back. I assumed I had to prove myself all over again immediately. Because that is what I learned. Love was conditional. Approval was temporary. Safety was never guaranteed.

Sit with that for a moment. If you cannot accept a compliment without feeling suspicious or uncomfortable, that is not a personality quirk. That is a family system pattern showing up in a workplace context. And it is exhausting. It is exhausting to be constantly bracing for the other shoe to drop. It is exhausting to assume that every kind word is a trap. It is exhausting to live in a state of hypervigilance, scanning for threats that are not there, preparing for rejections that never come.

What you can actually do about it

I am not going to tell you to quit your job. That is not always possible, and it is not always the answer. The pattern will follow you to the next workplace anyway, unless you address the pattern itself. So here is what I have learned from my own recovery and from the research of people like Deb Dana, who applies polyvagal theory to clinical practice.

First, you need to learn to recognize when you are being triggered versus when you are responding to an actual threat. This sounds simple. It is not simple. Because your body is really good at making the trigger feel like a real threat. The physical sensations are identical. The racing heart. The shallow breath. The tension. The urge to fight or flee or freeze. You have to learn to pause and ask: Is this situation actually dangerous? Or is my body treating it as dangerous because of something that happened a long time ago? That pause is everything. That pause is the space between the trigger and the response. And that space is where your freedom lives.

Second, you need to find ways to regulate your nervous system during the workday. This is not about breathing exercises that make you feel like a wellness influencer. This is about practical, discreet strategies that you can use at your desk. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). I keep one on my couch at home, and I use it after particularly triggering days. At work, I use smaller strategies. Pressing my feet into the floor. Noticing the temperature of the air on my skin. Looking at something far away. Orienting to the room. These are not woo-woo techniques. These are nervous system interventions. They tell your body: You are here. You are safe. This is not then. This is now.

Third, you need to start untangling the family patterns from the workplace patterns. This is the long work. This is the work that happens in therapy, in journaling, in honest conversations with people who get it. For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). Jonice Webb's work on childhood emotional neglect is a game-changer for understanding why you feel empty or unseen at work, why you cannot ask for help, why you assume your needs do not matter. Because if your family taught you that your needs did not matter, you will carry that into every relationship, including the one with your employer.

I am not going to tell you that this gets easy. It does not. I still get triggered. I still have days where I sit in my car and my hands shake. But I know what is happening now. I can name it. I can say to myself: This is a trauma response. This is my body remembering something that is not happening right now. And that knowledge gives me a tiny bit of distance. A tiny bit of choice. I can choose to respond differently. I can choose to speak up. I can choose to set a boundary. I can choose to ask for what I need. Even if my body is screaming at me to stay small and quiet and safe.

That is the work. Not to never be triggered. But to have the trigger and still be able to choose. To have the pattern and still be able to break it. To have the family system playing in the background and still be able to write a new script for yourself. That is possible. It takes time. It takes practice. It takes a lot of uncomfortable moments. But it is possible.

And you deserve that possibility. You deserve to not be eight years old every time your manager sends you an email. You deserve to bring your full adult self into the room. You deserve to be seen and heard and valued for who you actually are, not for the role you learned to play to survive.

That is what recovery looks like. Not perfect. Not linear. But real. And worth every uncomfortable step.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation and understanding the parts of yourself that formed to help you survive.
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Mark Wolynn's exploration of inherited family trauma and how patterns are passed down through generations.
  • YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link) - A weighted blanket that can help calm your nervous system after triggering days.
  • Running on Empty (paid link) - Jonice Webb's guide to understanding and healing from childhood emotional neglect.
